narrator: Nora, an Alaska public-radio host: warm, grounded, quietly witty
notes: Style: curiosity with earned, provisional competence, not cautionary and not booster. Warm forward-lean on the hook, then quiet pride building through the market stats and 'the plant planned ahead' (a co-op that saw it coming, credited plainly). A real, honest energy DROP at 'But North Pole already fails the federal air test' and through the verbatim quote, which is read grounded and plainspoken, like reading someone else's exact words aloud (Patrice Lee, a clean-air advocate) with weight and respect, never glossed. Then a level, hushed, undecided close on the last three lines: an honest open freeze, neither triumphant nor defeated. Dry wit only where earned (one wry aside), never sour. Pace: brisk, vary tone line to line. Emphasis: one key word per line, let the numbers land.
style_prompt:
Style: Warm, natural, conversational, quietly witty public-radio storytelling. The register is curiosity with earned, provisional competence: genuinely interested, crediting a co-op that planned ahead, and honest about the cost it cannot paper over. Not cautionary, not a booster. Open with warm curiosity and a forward lean. Build quiet pride through the market numbers and the line 'The plant planned ahead' (they saw it coming, say so plainly). Then let the energy genuinely DROP at 'But North Pole already fails the federal air test' and stay low through the quoted sentence. Read that quote grounded and plainspoken, as if reading someone else's exact words aloud, with real weight and respect, do not skate over it. Close level, hushed, and undecided on the final three lines: an honest open freeze, neither a triumphant button nor a defeated one. Dry wit only where the line earns it, never sour, never hype.
Pace: BRISK and energetic, keep it moving like a sharp modern explainer. Short natural breaths only, no long pauses, do not drag. Vary the tone line to line so no two sentences sound the same.
Emphasis: lean on the key word in each line; let numbers land.

lines.text: [curious] A power plant in North Pole is holding a ticket.
lines.why: Curious forward lean pulls the viewer in; 'ticket' is the odd, human image that opens the mystery of why a power plant holds one.

lines.text: Fifty thousand dollars, just to keep his place in line.
lines.why: Settle off the hook; land the figure, but emphasize 'line' so it sets up the reveal of what the line actually is.

lines.text: The line is a global turbine backlog, jammed by AI data centers.
lines.why: The flip from a local scene to a global cause; lift the energy from the line before so the reveal registers as a widening of scope.

lines.text: GE Vernova's backlog: near a hundred gigawatts.
lines.why: Slow slightly on the number; drop off the reveal so the figure has room to sit as evidence.

lines.text: [wry] A fifth of it, data centers.
lines.why: A quiet, quick aside that lets the proportion land dry; the low energy contrasts the stat before and coils for the bigger Siemens number.

lines.text: Siemens Energy: over sixty percent of this year's orders are data centers.
lines.why: The bigger number is the punch of the market section; lift back up so it lands harder than the wry aside before it.

lines.text: Fairbanks lost its cheap power in twenty twenty four, its gas deal in twenty twenty five.
lines.why: Grounded, a touch warmer: this is the honest local need that makes the co-op's move reasonable, not reckless.

lines.text: The plant planned ahead: a second turbine, ninety to one hundred twenty million dollars, plus a battery bet.
lines.why: Peak of quiet pride and competence; 'planned' carries the credit, the energy lift makes this the high point before the turn.

lines.text: But North Pole already fails the federal air test.
lines.why: The earned turn: a real drop from the pride peak, a beat before so the honest cost lands with weight, not as an afterthought.

lines.text: [short pause] "You cannot add to the pollution in a non-attainment area, and be legally correct."
lines.why: Lowest energy, grounded and plainspoken, a distinct register shift for reading someone else's exact words; the pause gives the verbatim quote room and respect.

lines.text: The board votes July twenty eighth.
lines.why: Come up just off the floor to a level, factual note; the decision is live and pending, stated cleanly, not sold.
Hushed and honest; the undecided freeze, dipping again so the close does not resolve into triumph or defeat.
A small lift and a beat before returns to the one human at the center; lands the open ending level, warm, and genuinely unresolved.

assembled_prompt:
Read ONLY the transcript below aloud as speech. The lines above "Transcript:" are direction; never speak them.
# AUDIO PROFILE: Nora, an Alaska public-radio host: warm, grounded, quietly witty
### DIRECTOR'S NOTES
Style: Warm, natural, conversational, quietly witty public-radio storytelling. The register is curiosity with earned, provisional competence: genuinely interested, crediting a co-op that planned ahead, and honest about the cost it cannot paper over. Not cautionary, not a booster. Open with warm curiosity and a forward lean. Build quiet pride through the market numbers and the line 'The plant planned ahead' (they saw it coming, say so plainly). Then let the energy genuinely DROP at 'But North Pole already fails the federal air test' and stay low through the quoted sentence. Read that quote grounded and plainspoken, as if reading someone else's exact words aloud, with real weight and respect, do not skate over it. Close level, hushed, and undecided on the final three lines: an honest open freeze, neither a triumphant button nor a defeated one. Dry wit only where the line earns it, never sour, never hype.
Pace: BRISK and energetic, keep it moving like a sharp modern explainer. Short natural breaths only, no long pauses, do not drag. Vary the tone line to line so no two sentences sound the same.
Emphasis: lean on the key word in each line; let numbers land.
Transcript:
[curious] A power plant in North Pole is holding a ticket.
Fifty thousand dollars, just to keep his place in line.
The line is a global turbine backlog, jammed by AI data centers.
GE Vernova's backlog: near a hundred gigawatts.
[wry] A fifth of it, data centers.
Siemens Energy: over sixty percent of this year's orders are data centers.
Fairbanks lost its cheap power in twenty twenty four, its gas deal in twenty twenty five.
The plant planned ahead: a second turbine, ninety to one hundred twenty million dollars, plus a battery bet.
But North Pole already fails the federal air test.
[short pause] "You cannot add to the pollution in a non-attainment area, and be legally correct."
The board votes July twenty eighth.
Nobody's decided.
Not even him.
